text:
Thank our Brother Baxter. Yes, sir. Good afternoon, friends. Very happy to be here to hear these stirring talks of these men: Mr. Jackson from South Africa. Just as I stepped behind the curtain there, just as I come in, I was--wanted to hear it. I was a, I guess a little too late for the dedication of the baby, but I was… Brother Baxter's give me some notes on people to be prayed for.
We want to thank our Lord for what He did last night. He was marvelous, and I just believe it'll be the--really, the exceedingly abundantly.
And I was glad to get to meet Brother Bosworth again. You know, we was talking about him last night, he and his son and them. And Brother Bosworth is feeling very much led that he's to go on ahead of us to South Africa. He wants to go down right away with a--to make arrangements down there and have some services down around Cape Town, Port Elizabeth, Grahamstown, Kler--Klerksdorp or--I guess I pronounced that right: Klarksdoff, and Bloemfontein, and through that part of the country there. And just before we come down… We're… According to the vision that the Lord has given me in--to return to Durban, and then from there to Bombay or somewhere up in India. And you just remember what I have told you last evening about the vision that the Lord gave, and see if it isn't just exactly that way.
text_no:
Takk til Bror Baxter. Ja, sir. God ettermiddag, venner. Svært glad for å være her og høre disse gripende talene fra disse mennene: Mr. Jackson fra Sør-Afrika. Akkurat da jeg gikk bak gardinen, rett etter at jeg kom inn, ønsket jeg å høre det. Jeg var, antar jeg, litt for sen til dåpen av babyen, men Bror Baxter har gitt meg noen notater om folk som skal bes i for.
Vi vil takke vår Herre for det Han gjorde i går kveld. Han var fantastisk, og jeg tror virkelig det vil være mer enn overflod.
Jeg var også glad for å møte Bror Bosworth igjen. Som dere vet, snakket vi om ham i går kveld, han og hans sønn og dem. Bror Bosworth føler seg sterkt ledet til å dra foran oss til Sør-Afrika. Han vil reise ned med en gang for å gjøre arrangementer der og ha noen møter rundt Cape Town, Port Elizabeth, Grahamstown, Klerksdorp (tror jeg uttalte det riktig), og Bloemfontein, og gjennom den delen av landet der. Og rett før vi kommer ned...
Ifølge visjonen Herren har gitt meg, skal vi returnere til Durban, og deretter videre til Bombay eller et sted i India. Og husk det jeg fortalte dere i går kveld om visjonen Herren ga, og se om det ikke blir akkurat slik.

text: Now, your contribution this afternoon, thank you so much for it, the Lord bless you. Brother Baxter did a very nice job of doing that, which he knows how to do it. We don't beg for money. Never… I wouldn't have a manager that would beg for money. When come time that our meetings won't pay out, it's time for me to go home. And we don't believe in that. And we've been amended by many of the brethren throughout, and in magazines, and so forth, of keeping the financial part down, saying nothing about it. We have to have finances to run the meetings, but there's none of it personally--just for our meetings. And we… And if I… Many and a many times have I dumped the love offering they give me in it. All that isn't just exactly what I have to eat… And people give me my clothes. And what I don't have to have, that goes right straight to foreign missions. And I know this: to be a steward over His welfare, if it goes to foreign missions, and I go myself to take the message that the Lord has give me, then at that day I'll know I've done the right thing with God's portion. And you…
text_no: Takk for ditt bidrag i ettermiddag; Herren velsigne deg. Bror Baxter gjorde en utmerket jobb med noe han er godt kjent med. Vi tigger ikke om penger, aldri... Jeg ville aldri hatt en manager som tigger om penger. Når tiden kommer at møtene våre ikke kan betale seg, er det på tide for meg å dra hjem. Vi tror ikke på det. Vi har blitt rost av mange brødre rundt omkring, og i magasiner, for å holde den økonomiske delen nede og ikke snakke om det. Vi trenger finanser for å drive møtene, men ingen av dem går til personlig bruk—kun til våre møter. Mange ganger har jeg puttet hele kjærlighetsofferet de gir meg tilbake i det. Alt som ikke er nødvendig for meg å spise... Og folk gir meg klærne mine. Alt jeg ikke trenger, går rett til utenlandsmisjoner. Jeg vet dette: Å være en forvalter over Hans velferd betyr at hvis det går til utenlandsmisjoner, og jeg selv tar budskapet som Herren har gitt meg, så vil jeg på den dagen vite at jeg har gjort det rette med Guds del. Og du...

text: Sometimes we go into a city, and we'd give it over to different organizations, and so forth. And we wouldn't get out of the city till they'd be calling us "holy-rollers, divine healers." And I give it to a certain organization one time, and I looked, going down the street in a great big Cadillac car, and a man with a big cigar and diamond rings, drawing about six--seven hundred dollars a month, just going around to distribute the money that… Well, that wasn't right. Poor little children, starving and hungry… And I know one thing, as Brother Jackson was saying them little natives back there, and so forth, never heard the Gospel, don't know nothing about Jesus… No. It's in my heart to take the Gospel to them. And the general orders was, "Go into all the world and preach the Gospel to every creature." And that's what this contribution, and everything that I… Every penny that I don't have to use just to keep my meetings a going, that goes right in till we get enough fund to go. And then away we go: Sweden, or wherever the Lord would lead.
text_no: Noen ganger reiser vi til en by og overlater arbeidet til ulike organisasjoner og lignende. Vi forlater ikke byen før de kaller oss "hellig-rullere" og "guddommelige helbredere." En gang overlot jeg arbeidet til en bestemt organisasjon, og jeg så en mann i en stor Cadillac med sigar og diamantringer, som tjente seks–syv hundre dollar i måneden kun for å distribuere pengene. Det var ikke riktig. Små, sultne barn led mens dette pågikk. Jeg vet én ting, slik Bror Jackson sa om de små barna og andre som aldri har hørt Evangeliet og ikke vet noe om Jesus... Det er i mitt hjerte å bringe Evangeliet til dem. De generelle påleggene var: "Gå ut i all verden og forkynn Evangeliet til all skapningen." Det er derfor bidragene og alt jeg ikke må bruke for å opprettholde møtene, går rett inn i midlene til vi har nok til å dra. Og så drar vi: til Sverige eller hvor Herren vil lede oss.

text:
Now, we're waiting now for South Africa and India. And on that great day, that when our Lord shall come and the dead in Christ shall rise, this mortal shall put on immortality, and we stand before Him to crown Him King of king and Lord of lords, I trust that every man and woman this afternoon, that even put a penny into that offering, that it'll be multiplied thousands of times for you in this life, and eternal life in the world to come. May God give it to you is my prayer. And with a… As a steward of His welfare, of His finance, I do the very best that I know how to keep it reverent and before God and to…
Soon as we get just enough… We don't get too much, because we… It's a great strain, that you realize how we have to hold the meetings. But we just go down, take the offering. Whatever we take, that's just all right. You see?
text_no:
Nå venter vi på Sør-Afrika og India. På den store dagen når vår Herre skal komme og de døde i Kristus skal oppstå, og dette dødelige skal ikle seg udødelighet, og vi står framfor Ham for å krone Ham til Kongenes Konge og Herrenes Herre, håper jeg at hver mann og kvinne denne ettermiddagen, som til og med har gitt en enkelt mynt i kollekten, skal få mangfoldig tilbake tusenvis av ganger i dette livet, og evig liv i det kommende. Må Gud gi Dere dette, er min bønn.
Som forvalter av Hans velferd og økonomi, gjør jeg mitt beste for å holde det høytidelig og for Gud. Når vi får nok, men ikke for mye, fordi det er en stor belastning, forstår Dere hvordan vi må holde møtene. Vi tar opp kollekten, og det vi får, er tilstrekkelig. Ser Dere?

text:
Now, Brother Bosworth, I said, is supposed to go ahead. He feels led of the Lord to go ahead of us down there to get some of those smaller towns, where we're coming back up into the other place, up into Durban. Pray for Brother Bosworth as he goes.
And I don't know just when He'll be sending me, but I know I am to go. You remember I told you the vision last night. I will have to go there, for the Lord is sending. And I'll do the very best I can to preach the Gospel. And I know Brother Bosworth is a very, very good teacher and will be quite able to do whatever he wants--or God wants him to do.
You know, it's strange, as I see him standing right here trying to speak to you, the Angel of the Lord is near, and I just seen a woman healed with stomach trouble, just right now, setting here in the meeting. That's right. That right? You had it, didn't you? Is that right? Raise up your hand. That's right. You're healed of it now. You just been healed while you were setting there.
Oh, there's many times, if I just speak what He shows, but I just seen that poor woman setting there. And I just looked around and she was so interested. When I said something about I was to being very reverent with the money to take it over there, she nodded her head some… She might know somehow. I don't know. And she was very sincere. I looked around and I seen a table move before her and seen her moving back like that. I knew it was stomach trouble. I looked back again, and I seen her eating then. I knowed she was healed and might as well tell her. That's right. So…
text_no:
Nå skal Bror Bosworth gå foran oss. Han føler seg ledet av Herren til å dra til noen av de mindre byene før vi kommer tilbake til Durban. Be for Bror Bosworth mens han drar.
Jeg vet ikke akkurat når Han vil sende meg, men jeg vet at jeg skal dra. Dere husker visjonen jeg fortalte om i går kveld. Jeg må dra dit fordi Herren sender meg, og jeg vil gjøre mitt aller beste for å forkynne Evangeliet. Jeg vet at Bror Bosworth er en svært dyktig lærer og vil være i stand til å gjøre det Gud vil ha ham til å gjøre.
Det er merkelig, mens jeg ser ham stå her og prøve å tale til dere, er Herrens Engel nær, og jeg så nettopp en kvinne bli helbredet fra mageproblemer. Stemmer det? Du hadde det, ikke sant? Er det riktig? Rekk opp hånden. Det stemmer, du er helbredet nå. Du ble nettopp helbredet mens du satt der.
Ofte viser Han meg ting, men jeg så akkurat den stakkars kvinnen sitte der. Jeg så rundt og hun var så interessert. Da jeg nevnte at jeg skulle være veldig nøye med pengene for å ta dem dit, nikket hun med hodet. Hun vet kanskje noe, jeg vet ikke. Hun var veldig oppriktig. Jeg så rundt igjen og så et bord bevege seg foran henne, og så henne lene seg tilbake. Jeg visste at det var mageproblemer. Jeg så tilbake igjen og så henne spise. Jeg visste at hun var helbredet og kunne like gjerne fortelle henne det. Det stemmer.

text:
Here's a dear person that's just been… The brother has been in an airplane crash and has been killed; it's Ben and Florence Smith. And here's another in here: "Special prayer, Brother Branham, for a man who's in the hospital and [unclear words] that's only forty-two…" All right. Let's bow our heads and pray for these while He's here now.
Heavenly Father, I pray that--as You look down here on this desk this afternoon and You see these people, oh, I think of that bereaved home of that man being killed in that crash. God, all things work together for good to them that love You. Trusting that his soul was right with You, and today he wouldn't return if he had to. If he was asked to, he'd take his choice; he'd stay where he's at. I pray that he was saved, Lord, that You'll comfort all his loved ones, and let them know this, that he said, "I would not have you ignorant, brethren, concerning those that are asleep, for we believe that Christ died and rose again the third day, even so, them that sleep in Christ shall God bring with Him." We believe that, Lord. He said, "Wherefore, comfort one another with these words." And we believe it's the truth.
And if this earthly tabernacle be dissolved, we have one already waiting to move into: one, not made with hands; but God Himself has made us a body, that when these spirits shall be set free from this one, we go to that glorious body. Realizing that one day that the blood of bulls and ox and sheep covered sin, but the blood of Jesus takes it away, and we go right straight to the presence of God. How we thank Thee for this. Oh, no wonder we can stand fearless, Lord, because that we have the vision. We have the assurance of the Holy Spirit, and He's here to vindicate and to prove God's power to us, that He loves us and is with us in these dark hours of the closing of the world's history.
This other man here, Lord, in the hospital, I pray that You'll heal him. May Your Spirit be upon him and deliver him this afternoon. And may his testimony cause others in that hospital just get up and walk out and be made well. Grant it, Father.
May there be an old-fashioned meeting, somehow, some way, Lord. Break through here in America and do great things before our Lord comes. For we ask it in Jesus' name. Amen.
text_no:
Her er en kjær person som nylig... Broren har vært i en flyulykke og har omkommet; det gjelder Ben og Florence Smith. Og her er en annen forespørsel: "Spesiell bønn, Bror Branham, for en mann som er på sykehuset og [uklare ord] som bare er førtito..." Greit. La oss bøye våre hoder og be for disse mens Han er her nå.
Himmelske Far, Jeg ber om at Du denne ettermiddagen ser ned på dette bordet og ser disse menneskene. Å, jeg tenker på det sørgende hjemmet til den mannen som ble drept i flyulykken. Gud, alle ting samvirker til det gode for dem som elsker Deg. Vi stoler på at hans sjel var i rett forhold til Deg, og at han i dag ikke ville ha returnert om han måtte velge; han ville bli der han er. Jeg ber om at han var frelst, Herre, og at Du vil trøste alle hans kjære, og la dem vite at han sa: "Jeg vil ikke, brødre, at dere skal være uvitende om dem som er sovnet inn, for vi tror at Kristus døde og oppsto den tredje dag. På samme måte skal Gud bringe med seg dem som sover i Kristus." Vi tror det, Herre. Han sa: "Så skal dere trøste hverandre med disse ord." Og vi tror det er sannheten.
Og om denne jordiske kroppen løses opp, har vi en allerede ventende: en som ikke er laget med hender, men som Gud Selv har laget. Når våre ånder frigjøres fra denne kroppen, går vi til den herlige kroppen. Vi innser at en gang dekket blodet av okser, sauer og kyr synden, men Jesu blod tar den bort, og vi går rett inn i Guds nærvær. Hvor takknemlige vi er for dette. Å, det er ikke rart vi kan stå fryktløse, Herre, fordi vi har visjonen. Vi har den Hellige Ånds forsikring, og Han er her for å bekrefte og bevise Guds makt for oss, at Han elsker oss og er med oss i disse mørke timene ved verdens slutt.
Denne andre mannen her, Herre, på sykehuset, jeg ber om at Du vil helbrede ham. Må Din Ånd komme over ham og befri ham denne ettermiddagen. Måtte hans vitnesbyrd få andre på det sykehuset til å stå opp og bli friske. Gi oss dette, Far.
Kan det bli et gammeldags møte, på en eller annen måte, Herre. Bryt igjennom her i Amerika og gjør store ting før vår Herre kommer. For vi ber om det i Jesu navn. Amen.

text:
How many is going to pray for Brother Bosworth as he goes on down? Let's see your hand. All right, Dr. Bosworth, see--I believe you're seated in the balcony. The Lord be with you. And these people will be praying for you and so will I. And then, will you be praying for us as we go down later on wherever the Lord will lead?
Now, when… Sometimes when you're debate here and witch doctors and everything else standing near, and skeptics and unbelievers and everything trying, first thing falls on my mind is, "My loved ones in America is praying." Somebody's bound to be praying, because we got literally hundreds and hundreds of thousands of people that pledged they would pray. And that many people, there's prayer going before God constantly for me (See what I mean?), constantly moving all the time. So that's the way…
text_no:
Hvor mange av dere vil be for Bror Bosworth når han reiser videre? La meg se hendene deres. Greit, Dr. Bosworth, jeg tror De sitter på balkongen. Herren være med Dem. Disse menneskene vil be for Dem, det samme vil jeg. Og vil De be for oss når vi drar videre dit Herren leder oss?
Noen ganger, når du debatterer her og heksedoktorer og alt annet står nær, og skeptikere og vantro prøver alt mulig, er det første som faller meg inn: "Mine kjære i Amerika ber." Noen må be, for vi har bokstavelig talt hundretusenvis som har lovet å be. Og så mange mennesker, det betyr at det alltid går bønner opp til Gud for meg (Skjønner dere hva jeg mener?), det beveger seg konstant. Slik er det...

text: The Angel of the Lord told me to go, and so that just makes it fearless to me. See, I know He's here. Now, you people setting here this afternoon, just… I don't know whether I'm going to speak or not. I… The Angel of the Lord's moving down this aisle again, there's… You believe I'm a fanatic? No, sir, I'm not. I'm telling you the truth. And your prayer cards that the boy gives out, that has nothing to do with your healing. This woman probably never seen a prayer card, knows nothing about anything about that, or any of the rest of you out in the audiences there that's been healed and knows that you don't have to have that. The only thing you have to have, is to believe what I'm telling you is the truth; and that is, Jesus Christ, the Son of God, that He rose from the dead, living among us today, just like He did those disciples down at Emmaus. He's living here. He does things. Now, those disciples had walked with Him all day and didn't recognize Him. But just the way He done something, they recognized that It was Him. Is that right?
text_no: Herrens Engel ba meg dra, og det gjør meg fryktløs. Ser dere, jeg vet at Han er her. Nå, dere som sitter her i ettermiddag, jeg vet ikke om jeg kommer til å tale eller ikke. Herrens Engel beveger seg ned denne midtgangen igjen. Tror dere at jeg er fanatisk? Nei, sir, det er jeg ikke. Jeg forteller dere sannheten. Og bønnekortene som gutten deler ut har ingenting med deres helbredelse å gjøre. Denne kvinnen har sannsynligvis aldri sett et bønnekort og vet ingenting om det, eller noen av dere andre i forsamlingen som har blitt helbredet og vet at det ikke er nødvendig. Det eneste dere må gjøre, er å tro at det jeg forteller dere er sannheten; og det er at Jesus Kristus, Guds Sønn, har stått opp fra de døde og lever blant oss i dag, akkurat som Han gjorde for disiplene på vei til Emmaus. Han lever her. Han gjør ting. Disiplene hadde gått med Ham hele dagen uten å gjenkjenne Ham. Men bare måten Han gjorde noe på, fikk dem til å forstå at det var Ham. Er det riktig?

text:
Now, you went to Sunday School this morning. You might've sang your songs, and studied your lesson, and so forth, and come back like all normal Christians do. But just looky here what He done just this afternoon, setting right here. Don't you realize that He's here? It's Him and He's here to make good anything that He has promised in His blessing.
Now, in the… I don't have time to preach. You realize that. I've just go thirty minutes. I want to use it for a testimony just to bring it right to a point for something, in a little text like, and try to watch my clock back there to be out at four-thirty, so you have time to get back here at seven-thirty for the services.
Now, tonight's the closing service. How many's going to be praying for great things tonight? That's wonderful. I try to get back around… I believe the boy told me that he'd be giving out the prayer cards around six thirty, or something like that. And come in; bring your sick and afflicted, and lay them up here close. And be praying for them. Don't worry about the prayer card; just be praying. And then God will do the rest of it when you just… Just be praying.
text_no:
I dag morges dro du kanskje til søndagsskolen, sang sanger, studerte leksen og så videre, og deretter kom tilbake som alle vanlige kristne gjør. Men se her hva Han har gjort bare i ettermiddag, akkurat her. Innser du ikke at Han er her? Det er Ham, og Han er her for å oppfylle alt Han har lovet i Sin velsignelse.
Jeg har ikke tid til å preke nå. Dere forstår det. Jeg har bare tretti minutter. Jeg vil bruke denne tiden til å vitne og komme til et poeng med en liten tekst, og prøve å følge klokka for å være ferdig til halv fem, slik at dere har tid til å komme tilbake klokken halv åtte til møtene.
I kveld er det avslutningsmøtet. Hvor mange vil be om store ting i kveld? Det er fantastisk. Jeg prøver å komme tilbake rundt... Jeg tror gutten sa at han ville dele ut bønnekortene rundt halv sju, eller noe sånt. Kom inn, ta med dere syke og plagerammede, og legg dem her foran. Og be for dem. Ikke bekymre dere om bønnekortet; bare be. Gud vil ta seg av resten når dere... bare ber.

text:
All right. In the second chapter of Saint Luke, a very familiar Scripture concerning the birth of our Lord, I want to read it just--not to follow up this missionary talk. Certainly not. But to, just to get a point that I believe that'll help you tonight. I… How many of you here is Christians, raise your hand? Oh, it looks like a whole hundred percent. That's wonderful.
Something happened in this meeting that's never happened before in my--any of my meetings. Right while the Angel of the Lord was a dealing with the people, He stopped and said, "Make an altar call." And I heard Brother Baxter, my brother who's a genius at that, give an altar call the night before--or I believe, maybe the same night. I don't know when it was. But I just walked in, and Billy had come out and met me and took me around to the room. And when I heard him make an altar call, and I walked out and about five people stood to accept Christ, and then right in the time, at that time where the Lord was moving out over the audience, then He stopped me and said, "Make an altar call." And thirty-some-odd came. See?
See, obedience, do what He tells you to do. No matter what it looks like, do what He says do and God will take care of the rest of it. Don't you think? Oh, if the church could only get to a place where we just forget about the routine, or whatever it is; just lose sense of time or anything else and just stay with God: do what He says do. Don't you believe the church needs a spiritual leading like that?
We've got our programs all drawed out, and lined out, and so forth. I don't believe it was ever intended for the apostolic church of God to be led by a program. I believe it's God's program to lead us by His Holy Spirit, just the way He will.
text_no:
I det andre kapittelet av Lukas-evangeliet, en velkjent skrift om vår Herres fødsel, vil jeg lese det kun for å fremheve et poeng som jeg tror vil hjelpe deg i kveld. Hvor mange av dere her er kristne? Løft hånden. Åh, det ser ut som hundre prosent. Det er fantastisk.
Noe skjedde i dette møtet som aldri har skjedd før i noen av mine møter. Mens Herrens Engel arbeidet med folket, stoppet Han plutselig og sa: "Gjennomfør en innbydelse til alteret." Jeg hørte Bror Baxter, min bror som er et geni på dette, gi en slik innbydelse kvelden før—or kanskje samme kvelden. Jeg er ikke sikker på hvilken kveld det var. Men jeg kom akkurat inn, og Billy hadde kommet ut og møtt meg og fulgt meg til rommet. Da jeg hørte ham gjennomføre en innbydelse til alteret, gikk jeg ut, og omtrent fem personer stilte seg opp for å ta imot Kristus. Da, midt i øyeblikket hvor Herren beveget seg utover publikum, stoppet Han meg og sa: "Gjennomfør en innbydelse til alteret." Og over tredve personer kom frem. Ser du?
Ser du, lydighet—gjør det Han sier du skal gjøre. Uansett hvordan det ser ut, gjør det Han sier, og Gud vil ta seg av resten av det. Er du ikke enig? Åh, hvis menigheten bare kunne komme til et sted hvor vi glemmer rutinen eller hva det måtte være; bare mister følelsen av tid eller noe annet og bare holder oss til Gud: gjør det Han sier. Tror du ikke menigheten trenger en slik åndelig ledelse?
Vi har programmene våre klare, oppstilt og så videre. Jeg tror ikke det noen gang var meningen at Guds apostoliske menighet skulle ledes av et program. Jeg tror det er Guds plan å lede oss ved Sin Hellige Ånd, akkurat som Han vil.

text:
Now, in the 23rd verse of the second chapter let's read. I believe that I have that right. Just a moment. No, it's the 25th verse of the second chapter.
…behold, there was a man in Jerusalem, whose name was Simeon; … the same man was just and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel: and the Holy Ghost was upon him.
And it was revealed to him by the Holy Ghost, … (Not the association; the Holy Ghost) … that he should not see death, before he'd seen the Lord's Christ.
And he came by the Spirit into the temple: and when the parents brought … the child Jesus, to do for him after the custom of the law,
Then took he him up in his arms, and blessed God, and said,
Now, Lord, now lettest … thy servant depart in peace, according to thy word:
For my eyes have seen thy salvation,
May the Lord add His blessings to this Word now, while… If you'll give me your undivided attention for about thirty minutes, by the clock back there, I'll try to have prayer and be dismissed. But I wished you would listen close now. I want to testify.
text_no:
La oss lese fra det 23. verset i det andre kapitlet. Jeg tror det er riktig. Et øyeblikk. Nei, det er det 25. verset i det andre kapitlet.
…se, det var en mann i Jerusalem som het Simeon; … denne mannen var rettferdig og from, ventende på Israels trøst, og Den Hellige Ånd var over ham.
Og det var blitt åpenbart for ham av Den Hellige Ånd, … (ikke foreningen; Den Hellige Ånd) … at han ikke skulle se døden før han hadde sett Herrens Kristus.
Og han kom drevet av Ånden inn i tempelet: og da foreldrene brakte … barnet Jesus, for å gjøre etter lovens skikk,
Så tok han Ham i sine armer, og priste Gud, og sa:
«Herre, nå lar Du Din tjener fare i fred, etter Ditt Ord,
For mine øyne har sett Din frelse.»
Må Herren legge Sin velsignelse til dette Ordet nå, mens… Hvis dere gir meg deres udelte oppmerksomhet i omtrent tretti minutter, skal jeg forsøke å be og avslutte. Men jeg ønsker at dere lytter nøye nå. Jeg vil vitne.

text:
Now, I want to testify by a text. I'm going to take the text and call it this: Expectancy: To Be Expecting Something.
Did you ever expect something? Usually, you get just what you expect. Did you know that? If you come to the meeting and would say, "Well, I'm going down there. There's nothing to it." Well, that's just what you'll get out of it. If you'd get down there and say, "Well, I--I'll go down there; but there's no need of me going because I--I ain't going to get healed," well, that's just the way you'll go back. But if you come expecting God to do something for you, He'll do it. If you expect to find something to criticize the meeting, criticize the way it's conducted, Satan will show you plenty of it. You'll find plenty. But if you come, not to criticize the meeting, you've come, saying, "God, I want to see where You are in it," God will show you Himself in it. Just whatever you expect, God will give it to you. It's your expectancy.
Now, I want you to come tonight expecting to see God move in the greatest way He's moved since we been here. Let's ask God not to leave one crippled person, one blind person, one deaf, one dumb; not one sick person, or crippled person in our midst tonight. Let's believe in it. Will you do it with me? And I believe God will do it, if we'll be expecting that now, for everybody to be healed.
text_no:
Jeg vil gjerne vitne gjennom en tekst som jeg vil kalle: Forventning: Å Forvente Noe.
Har du noen gang forventet noe? Som regel får du akkurat det du forventer. Visste du det? Hvis du kommer til møtene og sier: "Vel, jeg skal gå dit, men det er ingen ting i det," da er det nettopp det du vil få ut av det. Hvis du går dit og sier: "Vel, jeg går dit, men det er ingen vits i å gå fordi jeg ikke forventer å bli helbredet," da er det slik du vil gå tilbake. Men hvis du kommer med forventning om at Gud skal gjøre noe for deg, vil Han gjøre det. Hvis du forventer å finne noe å kritisere ved møtet, kritisere hvordan det er gjennomført, vil Satan vise deg nok av det. Du vil finne mye å kritisere. Men hvis du kommer uten å kritisere møtet og sier: "Gud, jeg vil se hvor Du er i dette," vil Gud vise Deg Selv i det. Uansett hva du forventer, vil Gud gi det til deg. Det er din forventning.
Nå vil jeg at du skal komme i kveld med forventning om å se Gud bevege seg på den største måten siden vi begynte her. La oss be Gud om at ikke én lam person, ikke én blind person, ikke én døv, ikke én stum; ikke én syk eller lam person skal være blant oss i kveld. La oss tro på det. Vil du være med meg? Og jeg tror Gud vil gjøre det, hvis vi nå forventer at alle skal bli helbredet.

text: Usually, as we're talking of expectancy, why, we think whatever we expect to do, that's what it'll be. And now, if I'm expecting to--I got an engagement or an appointment to meet you, I expect you to be there. And I know one thing; that we, every one today, got an appointment that we're going to meet God somewhere, sometime or other. Do you know that? "It was once appointed to man to die and after that the judgment." And we're all going to meet that. Some men's sins go before them, if you confess them here. Some follow after. So let's meet God now. Let's get the thing right and then be expecting Him when He comes in His glory and power, for Him to say, "It was well done, My good and faithful servant, enter into the joys of the Lord."
text_no: Vanligvis, når vi snakker om forventning, tenker vi at det vi forventer å gjøre, er det som vil skje. Hvis jeg for eksempel har en avtale om å møte deg, forventer jeg at du vil være der. En ting er sikkert; vi har alle en avtale om å møte Gud en dag, enten før eller senere. Vet du det? "Det er menneskene bestemt en gang å dø, og deretter dom." Vi skal alle møte det. Noen menneskers synder går foran dem, dersom de bekjenner dem her. Andre følger etter. Så la oss møte Gud nå. La oss få ting i orden, og deretter være forventningsfulle at når Han kommer i Sin herlighet og makt, vil Han si, "Det var vel gjort, Min gode og trofaste tjener, gå inn til Din Herres glede."

text:
Many things that I've noticed along the journey… I remember one thing, especially, when I was having a meeting. I'd just started off in my services. And I never will forget… My wife's not here this afternoon, so I can tell this all right. And we went down… I went to St. Louis first, where little Betty Daugherty was healed, where all the doctors of St. Louis had give her up. And I went there and prayed. And Mr. Daugherty, a very well known minister in the city, had just been revealed to me about two weeks before that. I didn't have money to go over on the train, and my congregation took up eleven dollars to buy my ticket over there and back. And I didn't have a coat, so one of my brothers let me have his coat to wear. And I went over on a chair car and got there the next morning. Mr. Daugherty standing out there, he was--he was expecting something. He said, "Brother Branham, I have heard that the Lord Jesus has visit you to show visions."
And I said, "That is right."
Said, "Do you know anything about my daughter?"
I said, "No, sir, I do not." We went up to their house and the little thing laying there screaming and going on. She weighed a… Just a little curly-headed girl, just in a terrible condition… And I said, "What does the doctor say?"
Said, "Oh, Brother Branham, I've spent money after money and none of them even can tell what it is." Said, "They think it's Saint Vitus' dance but they're not too sure." And said, "She's laid like this for weeks, and the churches has fasted and prayed, and we don't know what to do. And someone told me about…" How, I don't know who…
text_no:
På min reise har jeg lagt merke til mange ting... Jeg husker spesielt én hendelse da jeg hadde et møte. Jeg hadde nettopp startet med tjenesten min. Jeg vil aldri glemme det... Min kone er ikke her i ettermiddag, så jeg kan fortelle dette. Vi dro ned... Jeg dro først til St. Louis, hvor lille Betty Daugherty ble helbredet, etter at alle legene i St. Louis hadde gitt henne opp. Jeg dro dit og ba. Mr. Daugherty, en svært kjent forkynner i byen, hadde blitt åpenbart for meg omtrent to uker før dette. Jeg hadde ikke penger til å ta toget, så menigheten tok opp elleve dollar for å kjøpe billett til meg tur-retur. Jeg hadde heller ingen frakk, så en av mine brødre lot meg låne sin.
Jeg reiste i en sittevogn og var fremme neste morgen. Mr. Daugherty sto der og ventet ivrig. Han sa, "Bror Branham, jeg har hørt at Herren Jesus har besøkt deg for å vise visjoner."
Jeg svarte, "Det stemmer."
Han spurte, "Vet du noe om min datter?"
Jeg svarte, "Nei, sir, det gjør jeg ikke." Vi dro til huset deres, hvor den lille jenta lå og skrek og bar seg. Hun var en liten krøllete jente i forferdelig tilstand. Jeg spurte, "Hva sier legen?"
Han svarte, "Å, Bror Branham, jeg har brukt penger etter penger, men ingen av dem kan si hva det er. De tror det er Sankt Vitus' dans, men de er ikke sikre." Han fortsatte, "Hun har ligget slik i flere uker, og menighetene har fastet og bedt, men vi vet ikke hva vi skal gjøre. Og noen fortalte meg om…” Hvordan, vet jeg ikke hvem...

text:
And so, I went in and prayed for the little girl. All groups of people were in praying. We went down through the--from the parsonage to the church and prayed down there. And I went back. Nothing had happened. In them days, I didn't have meetings like this, of course. When I went to one case, I stayed there with that case till I found out what God was going to do about it. So then…
And I went on out in the yard and I walked up and down. I didn't know too much about St. Louis. I walked down the street a little ways and back. Hours passed, and I'd been there about eight hours then, and I was setting in Mr. Daugherty's car, and right out across the car a vision begin to move. Then I knew what would take place.
text_no:
Jeg gikk inn og ba for den lille jenta. Folk fra alle grupper ba. Vi gikk fra prestegården til menigheten og ba der. Så kom jeg tilbake, men ingenting hadde skjedd. På den tiden hadde jeg ikke møtene som nå. Når jeg tok en sak, ble jeg ved den til jeg fant ut hva Gud ville gjøre.
Så gikk jeg ut i hagen og vandret frem og tilbake. Jeg kjente ikke så godt til St. Louis. Jeg gikk litt nedover gaten og tilbake igjen. Timer gikk, og jeg hadde vært der i omtrent åtte timer. Jeg satt i Mr. Daughertys bil, og en visjon begynte å manifestere seg rett foran bilen. Da visste jeg hva som ville skje.

text:
Then Mr. Daugherty, the old father of Reverend Daugherty, come to the door, and he said, "Brother Branham, have you heard anything yet?" And young Mr. Daugherty was coming around the house.
I said, "Yes, I have 'Thus saith the Lord.'" He begin screaming and throwing up his hands. And I said… He was somewhere from… Their home was--had been up in Kentucky, but they'd moved up into St. Louis where he was sent by his church, I think, to take a charge there. And then I said, "I have 'Thus saith the Holy Spirit.'"
He said, "What must I do?"
I said, "Now, first go in and take everybody out of the house but your wife--all the people."
He said, "All right."
I said, "Don't doubt nothing now." And we went in, and we walked into the room, and there was the little girl lying there. I said to mother; I said, "Now, don't doubt anything. You go to your kitchen." I never seen where her kitchen was; it was a large house. And I said, "Down in the bottom of a drawer you'll find a little pan, like that, that you just bought about two days ago: blue granite. It's never had nothing in it."
She said, "Yes, sir, that's right."
I said, "Fill that about half full of clear water right out of the faucet and bring me a white handkerchief."
And she said, "All right."
She went and got it, and I said, "Now, Reverend Daugherty, you kneel down here on my right hand side at the foot of the bed, and father--the father, I said, "You, kneel down here," (the father of the Reverend Daugherty, the grandfather of the child); He did.
I said, "Now, Mrs. Daugherty, while I am repeating the Lord's Prayer, when I say, 'Our Father Who art in heaven,' you take that rag out of the pan, squeeze it out, and wipe it over her face. And then, about middle of the prayer, you wipe it across her hands. And then, at the end of the prayer, you wipe it across her feet, for 'Thus saith the Lord,' the devil that's got that child bound will leave when the last water's put on her feet." I said, "Don't doubt." And she… Why, her little tongue was all eat up and her lips and everything, her eyes sunk back… Weeks and weeks, and I think she'd been bedfast…
text_no:
Da kom herr Daugherty, den eldre faren til pastor Daugherty, til døren og sa: "Bror Branham, har du hørt noe ennå?" Samtidig kom den yngre herr Daugherty rundt huset.
Jeg svarte: "Ja, jeg har 'Så sier Herren.'" Han begynte å skrike og kaste hendene opp i været. Jeg sa... De hadde bodd i Kentucky, men hadde flyttet til St. Louis, hvor han var sendt av sin menighet, tror jeg, for å ha tilsyn der. Jeg sa: "Jeg har 'Så sier Den Hellige Ånd.'"
Han spurte: "Hva skal jeg gjøre?"
Jeg svarte: "Først må du ta alle ut av huset bortsett fra din kone."
Han sa: "Greit."
Jeg sa: "Ikke tvil på noe nå." Vi gikk inn i rommet, og der lå den lille jenta. Jeg sa til moren: "Nå, ikke tvil på noe. Gå til kjøkkenet ditt." Jeg hadde aldri sett hvor kjøkkenet hennes var; det var et stort hus. Jeg sa: "Nede i bunnen av en skuff finner du en liten panne, som du kjøpte for omtrent to dager siden: blå granitt. Den har aldri hatt noe i seg."
Hun sa: "Ja, det stemmer."
Jeg sa: "Fyll den halvfull med klart vann rett fra kranen og bring meg et hvitt lommetørkle."
Hun sa: "Greit."
Hun gikk og hentet det. Jeg sa: "Nå, pastor Daugherty, knel her på min høyre side ved fotenden av sengen, og faren—bestefaren til barnet—knel her." Det gjorde de.
Jeg sa: "Nå, fru Daugherty, mens jeg gjentar Fader Vår, når jeg sier 'Fader vår, du som er i himmelen,' tar du den kluten ut av pannen, klemmer den, og tørker den over ansiktet hennes. Rundt midten av bønnen tørker du den over hendene hennes. Ved slutten av bønnen tørker du den over føttene hennes, for 'Så sier Herren,' djevelen som har bundet barnet vil forlate når det siste vannet berører føttene hennes." Jeg sa: "Ikke tvil." 
Den lille jentas tunge var helt oppspist, leppene hennes og alt, øynene forsvunnet bakover... Ukesvis og ukesvis hadde hun vært sengebunden...

text:
If you want to write to him, that's Reverend Robert Daugherty, 2002 Gano Avenue, St. Louis, Missouri, if you want his [unclear words] you want his address to write. And so it was a, he went the… The little thing been there, I think, three months like that. Nothing could stop it; just screaming and clawing its little hair like that, and the streaks on its face and screaming; its mother all worn out.
So we knelt down. I said, "Our Father who art in heaven…" Mrs. Daugherty rubbed the little rag across her face. On down when I said--at the end of the prayer, to "Amen," when I said, "Amen," the little child was still screaming. Then I raised up, and I said, "Lord God, who created heavens and earth, who hath sent to pray for this child, in the Name of Jesus Christ, I ask the spirit of sickness that's on the child to leave." The little girl looked around, said, "Mommy, where are you?" And her mother begin screaming, dropped the pan, fell back in the floor like that. The father begin screaming and laid down in the floor. You'd have though he was a holy roller then (Uh-huh.), so then, the way he was carrying on. And that old dad, the grandfather, fell across the bed and begin to praising God. All formality had left then.
The little girl got up. I took her by the hand. I said, "Honey, what would you like to have?"
She said, "Who are you?"
I said, "I'm Brother Branham."
And she said, "I would like to have a--one of these milk shakes, you know."
I said, "Let's go get it." I just passed a drug store. With her little pajamas on, for the first time out of bed for three months, walked to the drug store and ordered two milk shakes and drank it with the child. And the doors were packed and jammed and the lawn around there. We had a meeting in St. Louis, over at the Kiel Auditorium, and the first night there, fourteen thousand people packed the thing out, and they had to turn them away: Expectancy. See what I mean?
text_no:
Hvis du ønsker å skrive til ham, er det til Reverend Robert Daugherty, 2002 Gano Avenue, St. Louis, Missouri. Hvis du vil ha adressen hans for å skrive. Så det var sånn at den lille tingen hadde vært der i omtrent tre måneder. Ingenting kunne stoppe den; bare skrikende og klorende i sitt lille hår, og med striper på ansiktet og skrikende; moren helt utslitt.
Vi knelte ned. Jeg sa: "Fader vår, du som er i himmelen…" Mrs. Daugherty gned den lille kluten over ansiktet hennes. Da jeg kom til slutten av bønnen og sa "Amen," skrek det lille barnet fortsatt. Så reiste jeg meg og sa: "Herre Gud, som skapte himmel og jord, som har sendt meg for å be for dette barnet, i Jesu Kristi Navn, ber jeg om at sykdomsånden på barnet må forlate." Den lille jenta så rundt seg og sa: "Mamma, hvor er du?" Moren begynte å skrike, slapp pannen og falt tilbake på gulvet. Faren begynte å skrike og la seg ned på gulvet. Du skulle tro han var en hellig ruller da, slik han oppførte seg. Den gamle bestefaren falt over sengen og begynte å prise Gud. All formalitet var borte da.
Den lille jenta reiste seg. Jeg tok henne i hånden og sa: "Vennen min, hva vil du gjerne ha?"
Hun sa: "Hvem er du?"
Jeg sa: "Jeg er Bror Branham."
Hun sa: "Jeg vil gjerne ha en av disse milkshakene, vet du."
Jeg sa: "La oss få det." Jeg hadde nettopp passert et apotek. Med lille pysjamasen sin på, for første gang ute av sengen på tre måneder, gikk vi til apoteket og bestilte to milkshakes og drakk dem sammen. Dørene var pakket og gressplenen rundt også. Vi hadde et møte i St. Louis, i Kiel Auditorium, og første kvelden var det fjorten tusen mennesker som fylte stedet, og de måtte avvise noen: Forventning. Ser du hva jeg mener?

text:
On down into Jonesboro, Arkansas… How many knows Richard T. Reed, at Jonesboro? Somebody in here. Somebody's got their hands up. Brother Reed… That fellow there, back over in here… Very fine man. Was you at the Jonesboro meeting when I was there? All right, here's a confirmation. Good.
We had several days of meetings. My wife had never seen one of the meetings like that: just my first going out. And the people had packed in there till they--I believe "The Sun" paper, "The Arkansas Sun," said there was twenty-eight thousand people that had entered the city. And if the paper said there's twenty-eight thousand, they were every one there. And so, they was just packed out into, for, as much as thirty and forty miles around the city. You couldn't even find a farmhouse that had a room to rent. And they'd had a places set up and food concessions, and the people in the literal building, the auditorium wouldn't even get out; they'd stay there, and they'd bring in, their loved ones, hamburgers. That's where I stayed for days and days and days trying to pray for all of them.
text_no:
Nede i Jonesboro, Arkansas... Hvor mange kjenner til Richard T. Reed i Jonesboro? Noen her inne? Noen har hendene oppe. Bror Reed... Den mannen der, borte i dette området... Veldig fin mann. Var du på møtet i Jonesboro da jeg var der? Greit, her er en bekreftelse. Godt.
Vi hadde flere dager med møter. Min kone hadde aldri sett et møte som dette; det var min første reise ut. Folk hadde pakket seg inn der; jeg tror "The Sun" avisen, "The Arkansas Sun," sa at det var tjueåtte tusen mennesker som hadde kommet til byen. Og hvis avisen sa tjueåtte tusen, da var de alle sammen der. De var pakket sammen opptil tretti og førti mil rundt byen. Man kunne ikke engang finne en bondegård med et ledig rom å leie. Det var satt opp plasser og matutsalg, og folk i selve bygningen, auditoriet, ville ikke engang gå ut; de ble der, og de fikk sine kjære til å bringe dem hamburgere. Jeg var der i dager og dager og prøvde å be for alle.

text:
And I remember the night, my wife came down. We was two city blocks away. The police was out there, just the streets was standing full. Not the auditorium, and the yards, and the fields, and the streets--everywhere, just standing, blocked with people, see if they could hear something going on. And when I got down there, four ushers was standing to get me down through the crowd. I never did know what become of my wife from there, how she got through there, how she got back. And we got to the place. And just as I got up to the place where the auditorium was, well, I come in on the platform.
And just as I come to the platform, I looked. A place had been roped off like that with nothing but just ambulance stretchers. Two nurses was standing here and a little girl dying with TB, about fifteen years old. She kept looking up at me. I knew that she was going get healed. I could tell the way, just the same as I knew that lady was going to be healed just then. Now, I knew she was going to be healed. And I kept watching her.
Just a few moments, I kept seeing somebody motioning like that, with a blue suit on, standing back that way. And it looked like a cab driver. I said, "Are you calling me, sir?"
He said, "Yes, sir." He said, "Brother Branham…" They had about fifteen ambulances backed up out there, the ambulance row. He said, "I have come down…"
text_no:
Jeg husker kvelden da min kone kom ned. Vi var to kvartaler unna. Politiet var der ute, og gatene var fulle av folk. Ikke bare auditoriet, men også gårdsplassen, åkrene, og gatene—overalt sto folk tettpakket for å høre noe som foregikk. Da jeg kom dit, sto fire kirkeverter klare til å lede meg gjennom mengden. Jeg vet fortsatt ikke hvordan min kone kom seg gjennom der, eller hvordan hun kom tilbake. Vi kom til stedet, og da jeg nådde plattformen i auditoriet, trådte jeg opp på den.
Da jeg sto på plattformen, la jeg merke til et avsperret område med bare sykebårer. To sykepleiere sto der med en liten jente på omkring femten år som holdt på å dø av tuberkulose. Hun holdt blikket på meg. Jeg visste at hun kom til å bli helbredet, akkurat som jeg visste at den damen kom til å bli helbredet tidligere. Jeg visste det og holdt øye med henne.
Et øyeblikk senere så jeg noen i en blå dress som vinket til meg fra avstand. Det så ut som en drosjesjåfør. Jeg spurte: "Kaller du på meg, sir?"
Han svarte: "Ja, sir. Brother Branham…" Det sto omtrent femten ambulanser oppstilt der ute, ambulanse etter ambulanse. Han fortsatte: "Jeg har kommet ned…"

text:
There'd been a man healed that day from Kennett--Clement, Missouri, I believe it was--up that boot-heel part of Missouri. The man had been totally blind for ten years, drawing a blind pension. And that night he was healed there at the meeting, and went back. Why, the next morning he got in home about daylight. He had his blind cane, his hat on the cane like that. He comes walking down through Methodist church a screaming and praising God (They throwed him out.), carrying on. He went over, run up into the Catholic church and turned him out. Why, he was about, set the city wild, everything.
And they took him out there and had him on the radio. He come through that little station down there, over from Jonesboro, I think, and… Oh, Bly--Blytheville, Blytheville station. You, I guess you, back there from Arkansas, know where that's at, the Blytheville station. That's where they were--his broadcasting had him on the radio that morning. He was a shoe cobbler there, years and years before that. And there he was, perfectly normal and well. And he could see. He stood right there in church and read the Bible and everything: had been totally blind; drawing a blind pension for ten years. And so they was just having an awful time.
text_no:
En mann fra Kennett, Missouri—jeg tror det var Clement—i den sørlige delen av Missouri ble helbredet den dagen. Mannen hadde vært blind i ti år og mottok pensjon for blinde. Den kvelden ble han helbredet på møtet og dro hjem. Neste morgen kom han hjem rundt daggry, med sin blindestokk og hatten hengende på den. Han kom gående gjennom en metodistkirke, ropende og prisende Gud (de kastet ham ut), og fortsatte å bære seg. Han gikk så til en katolsk kirke, hvor han også ble bortvist. Han gjorde hele byen oppskaket.
De tok ham med på radioen, gjennom en lokal stasjon der, over fra Jonesboro, tror jeg. Det var Blytheville-stasjonen. Dere som er herfra Arkansas vet sikkert hvor Blytheville-stasjonen er. De sendte hans vitnesbyrd på radioen den morgenen. Han hadde vært skomaker der i mange år før dette. Nå var han helt normal og kunne se. Han stod i menigheten og leste fra Bibelen og alt: han hadde vært fullstendig blind og mottatt pensjon for blinde i ti år. Det skapte stor oppstandelse.

text:
He… This man said, "I have brought patients down here. And I got a woman out here that's dying out here now, if she isn't already dead." And said, "I can't get the doctor nowhere," and says, "I--I don't know what to do." Said, "Can't you come to her?"
I said, "Brother, looky, there's probably a thousand people walled to that wall there? How could I get in there?"
And some men stepped out, said, "We'll take you if you want to go."
Brother Reed stepped up to the platform, said, "Go ahead." And out through there--and the pitiful part, them dear people… I ain't saying this because there's some Arkansas people setting here. See? But I tell you, they may not have too much of this world's goods, but they sure got faith that can make some of these big cities feeled ashamed of themselves. That's right.
They come down there. I seen young girls pack their shoes and stockings in their--in their hands, and be coming. They wouldn't know I was in woods there praying. And see them walk along the side of the road, barefooted, and then put their--dust off their feet and put their shoes and socks: young ladies, sixteen, seventeen years old, and go right on to the church like that. Come in old cotton wagons and things like that, trying to get…
text_no:
Han... Denne mannen sa, "Jeg har brakt pasienter hit. Jeg har ei dame her ute som ligger for døden, hvis hun ikke allerede er død." Han sa, "Jeg kan ikke få tak i legen noe sted," og sier, "Jeg--jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre." Han spurte, "Kan du ikke komme til henne?"
Jeg sa, "Bror, se, det er sikkert tusen mennesker som står inntil den veggen der. Hvordan skulle jeg komme meg inn der?"
Noen menn trådte frem og sa, "Vi kan ta deg hvis du vil gå."
Bror Reed gikk opp til plattformen og sa, "Gå i vei." Og ut der... og den triste delen, disse kjære menneskene... Jeg sier ikke dette fordi det er noen fra Arkansas som sitter her. Ser du? Men jeg må si, de har kanskje ikke mange av denne verdens goder, men de har tro som kan få noen av disse store byene til å skamme seg. Det er sant.
De kom ned dit. Jeg så unge jenter bære skoene og strømpene sine i hendene, og komme gående. De visste ikke at jeg var i skogen der og ba. Og se dem gå langs veikanten, barbeint, og deretter børste støvet av føttene sine og ta på seg sko og sokker: unge damer, seksten, sytten år gamle, og gå rett til menigheten på den måten. Kom i gamle bomullsvogner og slike ting, og prøvde å komme frem...

text:
And here, some time ago somebody wanted to give me a Cadillac car. And I said, "Do you mean to tell me that I have…" I said, "Brother, I'm glad you got one."
One said, "We just gave Avak one; why not give you one?"
I said, "Look, brother, you mean to tell me that I'd go down through Arkansas, and some of them poor little women out there pulling that cotton sack, and their back broke, and eating fat bacon, perhaps, and cornmeal for breakfast, and say, 'There goes Brother Branham,' going down the street out there in a Cadillac car?" I said, "Not me, brother. That don't run in my blood to do that." No, sir. If I got what I deserved, I'd ride a bicycle or be walking if it was going through there. No, that's right, brother.
But it's all right now, any of you's got a Cadillac, I ain't saying nothing against a Cadillac, but that's just… That's for you. See? All right.
text_no:
På et tidspunkt, for en tid tilbake, ønsket noen å gi meg en Cadillac. Jeg sa: "Mener du virkelig det?" og fortsatte, "Bror, jeg er glad du har en."
En annen sa: "Vi ga nettopp Avak en, hvorfor ikke gi deg en også?"
Jeg svarte: "Hør her, bror. Skal jeg kjøre gjennom Arkansas, hvor noen av de fattige kvinnene sliter med å bære tunge bomullssekker, med brukket rygg, og spiser spekk og maismel til frokost, og de ser 'Der går Brother Branham' i en Cadillac?" Jeg sa, "Ikke jeg, bror. Det ligger ikke i min natur." Nei, hvis jeg fikk som fortjent, ville jeg sykle eller gå gjennom der. Det stemmer, bror.
Men det er helt greit om noen av dere har en Cadillac. Jeg har ingenting imot en Cadillac, men det er bare... Det er for deg. Greit.

text:
Anyhow, in there, these poor people, laying out there… And I'd got to the ambulance, and he got me up there: and friends, one of the most pathetic sights I ever seen. There in that ambulance was an old dad down, the souls of his shoes out, patched… Oh, when he put me in mind of my own dad, his old blue shirt, faded and patched all over. An old hat in his hand was sewed with twine-cord, around like that, him saying, "O God, give her back to me. God, give her back to me," wringing his hands like that.
And the driver said, "Here's Brother Branham," called him by name. And I--and I…
"Oh," and he said, "Oh, Brother Branham, she's dead; she's dead." And he said, "Oh, mama's gone," started crying.
I said, "What is it, dad?" And I looked.
He said, "Look at her."
Well now, truly, I don't think the woman was dead. See? But her mouth was open to… Her teeth would been taken out, and she had… Her eyes were set way back, and like muddy water, like, run down the side. Her forehead was stiffened.
Now, I've seen people that was dead, and I seen the Lord bring them to life. If I had time I'd testify and tell you about it; but you've read it in the magazines and papers, and seen the notary seals and so forth that testify the same thing. I've seen three dead people that was pronounced dead, and laid out and gone, that's come back to life, because Jesus Christ did it. That's right. And…
text_no:
Uansett, der var disse stakkars menneskene, liggende der ute... Da jeg kom til ambulansen, fikk han meg opp dit. Venner, det var et av de mest hjerteskjærende synene jeg noen gang har sett. I den ambulansen lå en gammel far. Sålene på skoene hans var slitt ut, og klærne var lappede. Åh, han minnet meg om min egen far med den gamle blå skjorten, falmet og lappet overalt. En gammel hatt i hånden var sydd med hyssing, mens han sa: "O Gud, gi henne tilbake til meg. Gud, gi henne tilbake til meg," mens han vred hendene sine.
Sjåføren sa: "Her er Bror Branham," og ropte på ham ved navn. Og jeg—og jeg...
"Å," sa han, "å, Bror Branham, hun er død; hun er død." Han sa, "Å, mamma er borte," og begynte å gråte.
Jeg sa, "Hva er det, far?" Og jeg så på henne.
Han sa, "Se på henne."
Vel, nå, helt ærlig, jeg tror ikke kvinnen var død. Ser du? Men munnen hennes var åpen. Tennene hennes var fjernet, og øynene var sunket dypt tilbake, som om skitten vann rant ned langs siden av ansiktet hennes. Pannen hennes var stiv.
Jeg har sett folk som var døde, og jeg har sett Herren bringe dem til liv. Hadde jeg hatt tid, ville jeg vitnet om det; men dere har lest om det i magasiner og aviser, og sett notarialstempel og så videre som vitner om det samme. Jeg har sett tre døde mennesker, som var erklært døde, og lagt ut og gått bort, som kom tilbake til livet fordi Jesus Kristus gjorde det. Det er riktig. Og...

text:
But anyhow, in this case here, I think the woman was in a coma. I'm not sure. But I went up there and I took a hold of her. She was… I shook her, and she was laying…. And I said, "Can you--can you--can you hear me?" And her mouth was just open. She was laying stiff. I said, "Dad…" I had a hold of her hand. And I said, "Dad, let us--let's pray." And he said… I said, "God, please console the life of this poor man here, that his wife…"
He said, "Oh, Brother Branham, she was so sweet." Said, "She--she… We've raised our children." Said, "We done it. We horrowed over them old clods together, all through her life." Said, "We've worked so hard." And said, "And [unclear words] she took cancer." And he said, "When she got sick," said, "I--I sold my farm. I done everything to try to save her life." And said, "And I sold my mules." And said, "And I--I spent everything." And said, "The doctor's done the very best he could, but they couldn't stop it." And he said, "Brother Branham, how we come down here," said, "we sold her quilts that she's been quilting and making." Said, "And--and some of those blackberries that she canned last year," said, "we sold them to pay for the ambulance, bring them a hundred and fifty miles down." Said, "She's gone now, Brother Branham."
I said, "Well, dad, she was a Christian."
"Oh, yes, Brother Branham, she was a Christian."
I said, "Well, you'll meet her again."
text_no:
I denne situasjonen tror jeg kvinnen lå i koma. Jeg er ikke sikker. Jeg gikk bort til henne og tok tak i hånden hennes. Jeg ristet litt i henne, og hun lå der. Jeg spurte, "Kan du--kan du høre meg?" Munnen hennes var åpen, og hun lå stiv. Jeg sa, "Far..." Jeg holdt henne i hånden. Så sa jeg, "Far, la oss be." Han svarte... Jeg fortsatte, "Gud, vær så snill og trøst denne stakkars mannen her, hans kone..."
Han sa, "Å, Bror Branham, hun var så søt." Han fortsatte, "Vi har oppdratt barna våre." Han sa, "Vi gjorde det. Vi pløyde jorda sammen, hele hennes liv." Han la til, "Vi har jobbet så hardt." Så sa han, "[Utydelige ord] hun fikk kreft." Da hun ble syk, sa han, "Jeg solgte gården min. Jeg gjorde alt jeg kunne for å redde livet hennes." Han sa, "Jeg solgte muldyrene. Jeg brukte alt." Videre sa han, "Legen gjorde sitt aller beste, men de kunne ikke stoppe det." Han sa, "Bror Branham, vi kom hit ned," og fortsatte, "vi solgte teppene hun hadde laget." Han la til, "Og noen av bjørnebærene hun hermetiserte i fjor," sa han, "vi solgte dem for å betale for ambulansen, så hun kunne komme seg hit de 150 milene." Videre sa han, "Nå er hun borte, Bror Branham."
Jeg sa, "Vel, far, hun var en kristen."
"Å ja, Bror Branham, hun var en kristen."
Jeg sa, "Vel, du vil møte henne igjen."

text:
I said, "Let's pray." And we got to praying. And as I was praying, like that, I said, "Lord God, You made heavens and earth. You know all things. I pray that You'll console. Thou art God. I know not what to say." And about that time I felt something moving down. I thought, "Why, that's just psychology. I'm just thinking that." And I just kept on. I said, "Lord God, You know all things; all thing's committed to You." And about that time, I feeled her hand twitch on mine. Now.
Well, Satan said, You know what that was?" Said, "That's… She's dying; that's her nerves jumping like that." But all the time I could feel the vibration of that cancer. And now, the reason I thought--know she--didn't think she was dead, because that cancer would've went with it; but the germ of cancer was still there. I knew it.
In a few moments, the cancer ceased. It didn't move no more. And I kept holding her hand and praying. After while I looked down; she squeezed my hand. I knowed that wasn't nerves twitching. So he was still crying and praying. I looked over at him. And when I looked over at him, he was just crying and praying. I looked down to her, and this skin on her forehead was wrinkling back, and she was moving her eyes like that. I just held real still. He just kept praying just as loud as he could, just screaming and crying and crying, "God, give her back."
And she looked over; she said, "Who are you?"
I said, "I'm Brother Branham."
About that time he looked up, and he heard that, and he looked; he hollered, "Mother, Mother, Mother," and he grabbed her in his arms like that and begin to screaming.
About a year after that, she was testifying on the radio about it in one of my programs down in Texas somewhere there where she come down, given a testimony.
text_no:
Jeg sa: "La oss be." Og vi begynte å be. Mens jeg ba, sa jeg: "Herre Gud, Du skapte himmelen og jorden. Du kjenner alle ting. Jeg ber om at Du skal trøste. Du er Gud. Jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal si." Omtrent på den tiden følte jeg noe bevege seg nedover. Jeg tenkte: "Nei, det er bare psykologi. Jeg bare innbiller meg det." Jeg fortsatte å be og sa: "Herre Gud, Du kjenner alle ting; alt er overgitt til Deg." Plutselig merket jeg at hun rykket i hånden min.
Satan sa: "Vet du hva det var?" Han sa: "Hun dør; det er nervene hennes som hopper slik." Men hele tiden kunne jeg føle vibrasjonen fra kreften. Grunnen til at jeg ikke trodde hun var død, var at kreften ville ha forsvunnet med henne; men kreftens bakterie var fortsatt der. Jeg visste det.
Etter noen minutter, sluttet kreften å bevege seg. Jeg holdt fortsatt hånden hennes og ba. Etter en stund klemte hun hånden min. Jeg visste at det ikke var nervetrekninger. Han gråt og ba fortsatt. Jeg så på ham, og han bare gråt og ba høyt. Jeg så ned på henne, og merket at huden på pannen hennes rynket seg tilbake, og hun beveget øynene. Jeg holdt meg helt stille. Han fortsatte å be høyt, skrikende og gråtende: "Gud, gi henne tilbake."
Hun så på meg og sa: "Hvem er du?"
Jeg svarte: "Jeg er Bror Branham."
Akkurat da så han opp, hørte det og ropte: "Mor, Mor, Mor," og han tok henne i armene sine og begynte å rope.
Omtrent et år etter dette, vitnet hun på radioen i ett av mine programmer, et sted nede i Texas, hvor hun kom ned og ga vitnesbyrd.

text:
Well, I said, "Well, I'm going to get back now to the car."
And that man said, "My, Brother Branham, there's two thousand people packed between here and that door." Said, "You couldn't get back if you had to." Said, "Now look, the men that brought you," said, "I sent them around at the back in a big parking lot. You know where that is, back behind the place there." And he said, "Now, nobody knows you back there," which they had been standing back there, but nobody got in. Said, "Now, you go down this way, go through this ambulance row, and come back around. There's an alley like that comes in behind that lot; then you come up into the place there's flood lights. Said, "You go on up over to the corner, and they're trying to twist their way through those people.
And I said, "Why, they'd see me get out."
He said, "I'll take my coat off and hold it up to the door like this so they won't see you." Now, that looked like awful to do that, but I climbed over the seat and went on out the front way, side; went up through that row of ambulances there and come on up and started going through. And I was pushing people this way and that way (You know?), getting in. Was kind of drizzling rain.
Somebody said, "Stop pushing."
I said, "Yes, excuse me." I just kept on pushing, go right through them, going on.
Someone said, "Set down." I just kept on moving (You know?), everybody…
And after while I pushed up against a great big typical Arkansawyer. "Excuse me." Aha. And he said. And I pushed up against him; he's standing there whittling with a knife. And I… Somebody bounced me over against him. I said… He said, "Set down."
And I said… I was afraid he was going to set me down, so… I said, "Excuse me, sir, I didn't mean to do that."
Said, "Don't you know no better than to push like that?"
I said, "Yes, sir, I do, but I'm very sorry."
He turned around and kept on whittling, and said, "As I was saying…" (You know?), talking like that.
And I thought, "Good."
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Vel, nå skal jeg komme meg tilbake til bilen."
Mannen svarte: "Bror Branham, det er to tusen mennesker mellom her og døren. Du kommer deg ikke tilbake om du må." Han fortsatte: "De som kjørte deg hit, er sendt rundt til baksiden der det er en stor parkeringsplass. Du vet hvor den er, bak ved bygningen." Og han sa: "Ingen kjenner deg der bak, men de står der allerede og venter. Gå ned denne veien, forbi rekken med ambulanser, og kom inn bakveien til parkeringsplassen; derfra vil du se flomlysene. Gå så til hjørnet, og de prøver å bane seg vei gjennom folkemengden."
Jeg sa: "Men de vil se meg komme ut."
Han sa: "Jeg tar av meg jakken og holder den opp mot døren slik at de ikke ser deg." Selv om det virket litt drastisk, klatret jeg over setet og gikk ut fronten, langs siden; forbi ambulanserekken og videre. Jeg dyttet folk til siden (du vet), og det regnet litt.
En sa: "Slutt å dytte."
Jeg svarte: "Beklager," og fortsatte å dytte meg frem.
En annen sa: "Sett deg ned." Jeg ignorerte det og fortsatte å bevege meg.
Så støtte jeg på en stor mann fra Arkansas. "Unnskyld," sa jeg. Han sto der og spikket med en kniv. Noen dyttet meg borti ham.
Han sa: "Sett deg ned."
Jeg fryktet at han faktisk ville sette meg ned, så jeg sa: "Unnskyld, sir, det var ikke meningen."
Han sa: "Vet du ikke bedre enn å dytte slik?"
Jeg svarte: "Jo, sir, det vet jeg, og jeg beklager."
Han snudde seg og fortsatte å spikke, mens han sa: "Som jeg sa..." (du vet), fortsatte samtalen.
Jeg tenkte: "Bra."

text:
So I watched him a little bit. You know? And after while I heard somebody hollering, "Daddy! Daddy!"
I thought, "Where is that?" I looked coming down through the crowd. Now, the Jim Crowe law is in Arkansas: segregation, colored's not allowed with the white. So you know that. So then… And I looked, coming down through there, and there come a young colored girl. She looked to be about in her late teens, very well dressed. And she was just as blind as she could be. Her eyes was white with cataracts as my shirt. And she was pushing along like that, hollering, "Daddy! Daddy!" no one noticing her.
Well, I thought, "That poor thing." And I thought, "I can do something for her, maybe. And I looked around; I couldn't see the men. They'd never got around that corner yet to where the building, so I… It was big great lot in there, way larger than this place in here--about a half a block, city block back there. So I--and I was standing there watching. And she started coming this way, pushing and bumping everybody, excusing herself, and going on hollering, "Daddy! Oh Daddy! Where are you?"
So I just leaned over. It looked kind of…
text_no:
Jeg observerte ham en stund. Etter en stund hørte jeg noen rope: "Pappa! Pappa!" Jeg tenkte: "Hvor kommer det fra?" Jeg så mot folkemengden. Nå, i Arkansas gjelder Jim Crow-lovene: segregering, fargede har ikke lov til å være sammen med de hvite. Det vet dere.
Jeg så mot lyden, og der kom en ung farget jente. Hun virket å være i slutten av tenårene, meget pent kledd. Men hun var helt blind. Øynene hennes var hvite av grå stær, som skjorten min. Hun famlet seg frem og ropte: "Pappa! Pappa!" uten at noen la merke til henne.
Jeg tenkte: "Stakkars jente." Og jeg tenkte: "Kanskje jeg kan gjøre noe for henne." Jeg så meg rundt, men kunne ikke se mennene. De hadde ennå ikke kommet rundt hjørnet der bygningen var. Området var stort, mye større enn dette stedet her—omtrent en halv byblokk.
Jeg sto der og observerte. Hun begynte å komme denne veien, skyvende og støtende borti folk, unnskyldende seg, og fortsatte å rope: "Pappa! Å pappa! Hvor er du?" Jeg lente meg over. Det så litt…

text:
Now, you… Now, wait till this is over, because you'll think I was a hypocrite doing this. So I moved over to where I got right in the line; when she'd go this way, I'd stay this way till she bumped into me. And she bumped into me like that. Something told me go over there. And she hit me. She said, "Pardon me."
I said, "Yes, ma'am."
She said, "Daddy!"
I said, "What do you want? Who you calling?"
She said, "Sir," (That's Southern talk. You know?); she said, "Sir, I want my daddy. I can't find him nowhere." Said, "I--I just don't know who… Nobody will help me."
I said, "Where you from?"
She said, "I's from Memphis." That's about eighty miles. I looked out there at those chartered buses and I seen one said, 'Memphis.'
I said, "What're you doing over here?"
She said, "I come to see the healer."
Said, "The what?" Thought I'd just try her faith. Now, that looks horrible to do that.
And she said, "I come to see the healer." She said, "They tell me this is his last night here, and I can't even get near the building. I lost my daddy. Nobody will help me back to the bus, and I don't know what to do. Will you help me, kind sir?"
And I said, "I want to question you just a minute. You said you come to see who?"
She said, "The healer."
I said, "Do you believe such stuff as that?"
She said, "Yes, sir."
And I thought… Well, that kind of made me feel little… You know? And I said, "Well, what do you…? How…? You believe he could--he could help you?"
She said, "Yes, sir." She said, "Look, the…" She said, "I's was a little girl about ten years old. I got cataracts on my eyes. The doctor told me when they got ripe, he would take them out." Now, what 'ripe' means, I don't know. But said, "When they got ripe, he'd take them out." Said, "Now, they ripe, and the doctor says [Brother Branham coughs.] (Pardon me.) if he would take them out, he'd pull the optical nerves out of my eyes. And I can't never be healed until I go in the building and see him.
text_no:
Vent til dette er over, ellers vil du tro jeg var en hykler ved å gjøre dette. Jeg beveget meg over til der jeg kom midt i linjen; når hun gikk den ene veien, ble jeg stående til hun støtet på meg. Og hun støtet på meg slik. Noe sa til meg at jeg skulle gå dit. Da hun traff meg, sa hun: "Unnskyld meg."
Jeg svarte: "Ja, frue."
Hun sa: "Pappa!"
Jeg spurte: "Hva vil du? Hvem kaller du på?"
Hun svarte: "Sir," (Det er sørlig talemåte, vet du); hun sa, "Sir, jeg vil finne pappa. Jeg kan ikke finne ham noe sted." Hun sa, "Jeg... Jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre... Ingen vil hjelpe meg."
Jeg spurte: "Hvor kommer du fra?"
Hun svarte: "Fra Memphis." Det er omtrent åtti mil unna. Jeg så på de chartrede bussene og så en som sa ‘Memphis.’
Jeg spurte: "Hva gjør du her?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg kom for å se helbrederen."
"Helbrederen?" Trodde jeg skulle prøve troen hennes, selv om det ser forferdelig ut å gjøre det.
Hun sa: "Jeg kom for å se helbrederen. De sier dette er hans siste kveld her, og jeg kan ikke engang komme nær bygningen. Jeg har mistet pappa. Ingen vil hjelpe meg tilbake til bussen, og jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre. Vil du hjelpe meg, snille sir?"
Jeg svarte: "Jeg vil stille deg noen spørsmål et øyeblikk. Du sa du kom for å se hvem?"
Hun svarte: "Helbrederen."
Jeg spurte: "Tror du på slike ting?"
Hun sa: "Ja, sir."
Det fikk meg til å føle meg liten, vet du, og jeg spurte: "Du tror han kan hjelpe deg?"
Hun sa: "Ja, sir." Hun fortsatte: "Da jeg var en liten jente på omtrent ti år, fikk jeg grå stær på øynene. Legen sa at når de var modne, ville han ta dem ut." Jeg vet ikke hva 'modne' betyr i denne sammenhengen, men hun sa: "Når de ble modne, ville han ta dem ut." Hun sa: "Nå er de modne, og legen sier [Bror Branham hoster.] (Unnskyld meg.) hvis han tar dem ut, vil han rive de optiske nervene i øynene mine. Jeg kan aldri bli helbredet før jeg går inn i bygningen og ser ham."

text:
I said, "Well, woman, do you believe any such stuff as that, in the days that we got these fine doctors and medical science and things, and do you believe that that story about that Angel appearing to that man is the truth?"
She said, "Yes, sir."
And I said… Now, she couldn't see me, because she's totally blind. And I said--I said, "How'd you ever hear about this?"
She said, "On the radio this morning, I heard a man testifying, had been blind." So that was the man from ten years, up there. Said, "I heard him testify and heard him read the Bible." And she said, "Sir, that's the only hope I have to ever see."
And I said, "Do you mean you believe such?"
She said, "Listen, sir, is there any way that you could get me to where he's at?" Said, "If you'll get me where he's at, I'll find my daddy after that." Oh, my. That was too much for me. "I'll find my daddy if I can get in there." Expectancy, if she could ever get where God was moving, something's going to happen.
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Vel, kvinne, tror du virkelig på slikt, i vår tid med dyktige leger, medisinsk vitenskap og alt det der? Tror du på historien om den Engelen som viste seg for den mannen, er sann?"
Hun sa: "Ja, sir."
Jeg sa... Nå, hun kunne ikke se meg, for hun var helt blind. Jeg sa: "Hvordan fikk du høre om dette?"
Hun sa: "På radioen i morges hørte jeg en mann vitne; han hadde vært blind." Det var mannen fra ti år tilbake. Hun fortsatte: "Jeg hørte ham vitne og høre ham lese fra Bibelen." Så sa hun: "Sir, det er mitt eneste håp om å kunne se."
Jeg spurte: "Mener du at du virkelig tror på dette?"
Hun svarte: "Hør her, sir, finnes det noen måte du kan få meg dit hvor han er?" Hun sa: "Hvis du kan få meg dit han er, skal jeg finne min far etterpå." Å, det ble for mye for meg. "Jeg skal finne min far hvis jeg kan komme dit." Forventningen om at hun kunne komme der hvor Gud virket, gjorde henne overbevist om at noe kom til å skje.

text:
I thought of that of that blind man, this mor--that morning. Then I thought of old blind Fanny Crosby:
Pass me not, O gentle Saviour,
Hear my humble cry;
While on others Thou are calling,
Do not pass me by.
Thou the Stream of all my comfort,
More than life to me,
text_no:
Jeg tenkte på den blinde mannen den morgenen. Så tenkte jeg på den gamle, blinde Fanny Crosby:
Pass me not, O gentle Saviour,
Hear my humble cry;
While on others Thou art calling,
Do not pass me by.
Thou the Stream of all my comfort,
More than life to me,

text:
I thought, "That poor, blind Ethiopian girl, standing there, how she was trying…" If one blind man could receive his sight, why couldn't she? And there I felt sorry for her. I said, "Look; be real quiet now, will you?"
And she said, "Yes, sir. Will you go take me in?"
And I said, "Just a moment now. Now, nobody knows we're talking." I said, "Do you want to… Who'd you say you want to see?"
She said, "The healer."
I said, "Look. Do you mean, you want to see Brother Branham?"
She said, "That's him."
I said, "I am Brother Branham." Said…. And she grabbed me like that, and she said… I said, "Look, turn me loose. Turn…"
"Oh, no. No, no." She held me like that. Said, "No." She said, "Is you the healer?"
I said, "No, I'm Brother Branham. Jesus is the Healer."
She said, "Well, you's the man that prayed for that man that got… Oh…" She said, "Thank You, Lord. Thank You, Lord," just holding me like that, right by the lapels of the coat.
I said, "Look, lady, I want to take a hold of your hand," but there's no way of doing that. I couldn't pry her hands loose. I'd pull like that, and she'd pull my coat. And I didn't want to attract attention, so I just got a hold of her hands like this and held them. I said, "Now, you bow your head and don't say nothing. Keep quiet, see," I said, "because the people will push in here, and then I won't be able to pray for you."
And she said, "I hear you. I hear you."
And I said, "Now, bow your head and you believe that Jesus is going to give you your sight?
She said, "I know He is now."
text_no:
Jeg tenkte, "Den stakkars blinde etiopiske jenta som står der, hvordan hun prøver..." Hvis én blind mann kunne få synet tilbake, hvorfor kunne ikke hun? Jeg følte virkelig medlidenhet for henne. Jeg sa, "Se, vær helt stille nå, vil du?"
Hun sa, "Ja, herr. Vil du følge meg inn?"
Jeg sa, "Et øyeblikk nå. Nå er det ingen som vet at vi snakker sammen." Jeg sa, "Vil du... Hvem sa du at du vil møte?"
Hun sa, "Helbrederen."
Jeg sa, "Se. Mener du at du vil møte Bror Branham?"
Hun sa, "Det er ham."
Jeg sa, "Jeg er Bror Branham." Da... Og hun grep tak i meg, og hun sa... Jeg sa, "Se, slipp meg. Slipp..."
"Å, nei. Nei, nei." Hun holdt fast i meg. Hun sa, "Nei." Hun sa, "Er du helbrederen?"
Jeg sa, "Nei, jeg er Bror Branham. Jesus er Helbrederen."
Hun sa, "Vel, Du er mannen som ba for den mannen som fikk... Åh..." Hun sa, "Takk Deg, Herre. Takk Deg, Herre," mens hun holdt fast i meg, rett ved jakkeslaget.
Jeg sa, "Se, frue, jeg vil ta hånden Din," men det var umulig. Jeg klarte ikke å få hennes hender løs. Når jeg trakk, trakk hun i jakken min. Jeg ville ikke tiltrekke meg oppmerksomhet, så jeg tok bare tak i hendene hennes og holdt dem. Jeg sa, "Nå, bøy hodet og si ingenting. Hold deg stille, se," sa jeg, "for ellers vil folk presse seg inn her, og da kan jeg ikke be for deg."
Hun sa, "Jeg hører Deg. Jeg hører Deg."
Jeg sa, "Nå, bøy hodet og tro at Jesus vil gi Deg synet tilbake?"
Hun sa, "Jeg vet at Han vil nå."

text:
And so she bowed her head like that. And I… Here's the prayer I prayed. I said, "Almighty God, the Creator of heavens and the earth, have respect to this poor black girl's prayer." I said, "Some nineteen hundred years ago, there was an old rugged cross going down through Jerusalem, dragging out the bloody footprints of the Bearer. On His road up the hill, His little frail body, with such a load, fell. He couldn't go no farther. He was bleeding; a crown of thorns on His head; and His shoulders rubbed; His back bleeding; striped and wounded; the blood pouring out down along the street; His little frail body fell. Simon, the Ethiopian, a colored man, come along and picked up the cross and said, 'I helped Him bear the cross.'" I said, "Dear God, You remember that, Lord Jesus." I said, "Here's one of His children, staggering in total darkness. And I don't know why You sent me around beside this building here, but they're taught the footsteps of a righteous man is ordered by the Lord. Then, Lord, all I know to do is to ask You. I pray that You'll give this poor girl her sight. Grant it, Lord."
The vibration of the cancer, or the tumors on her eyes; cataracts stopped. That's the only way I had of knowing in them days. You know when that was going on, testified that this other would come. I knew she was healed then. I said, "Now, keep your head bowed." I said, "Don't you raise your head till I tell you now. Now, you keep your eyelids closed."
She said, "Yes, sir." She said, "I feel real cool in my eyes."
I said, "Just keep your head bowed." I said, "Now, raise your head till just about where you think you'll look right about even with me."
She said, "Yes, sir." Said, "That about right?"
And I said, "That's about right?" I said, "Now, in the name of the Lord Jesus, receive your sight. Open your eyes."
She said, "Is that lights?"
And I said, "Yes."
Said, "What's them black--them spots passing by me?" Said, "Is that people?"
I said, "Yes."
She said, "Oh, Lord, I who was once blind, can now see." Oh, my, she attracted the whole country around. She screamed and jumped up in the air.
text_no:
Hun bøyde hodet slik. Jeg ba denne bønnen: "Allmektige Gud, Himmelens og jordens Skaper, ha medlidenhet med denne fattige svarte jentas bønn. For noen nitten hundre år siden, var det et gammelt, ruglete kors på vei gjennom Jerusalem, som dro ut de blodige fotsporene til Bæreren. På vei opp bakken falt Hans lille, skrøpelige kropp i tyngden av korset. Han kunne ikke gå lenger. Han blødde; en krone av torner på Hodet; Skuldrene gnidd; Ryggen blødende; stripet og såret; blodet rant nedover gaten; Hans skrøpelige kropp falt.
